title: Understanding the lifetime of water with dynamic network analysis: the case of CsOH.H2O
authors: Graeme J. Ackland, Ciprian G. Pruteanu, John S. Loveday, Keishiro Yamashita
abstract: We describe the atomic-level motions in caesium hydroxide monohydrate (CsOH$\cdot$H$_2$O), which is a chemical compound containing layers of water and hydroxide ions. At this composition, each oxygen is involved in three hydrogen bonds which, in the hexagonal structure, form a quasi-2D honeycomb lattice. While oxygen and caesium atoms form a typical crystal lattice, the dynamics of the hydrogen atoms are more complex. Here we show that the covalent and hydrogen bonds are continually interconverting, meaning that the water and hydroxyl are interconverting by proton exchange. The order-disorder transition of the water and hydroxyl proceeds by chemical reaction rather than rotation or diffusion of the molecules. A hydrogen can rotate out of the layer, leaving a vacant site in the 2D layer. Such a hydrogen vacancy can diffuse rapidly by single molecule rotation, leading to fast-ionic conduction. The proton exchange leads to a novel type of Raman activity combining stretch and exchange processes, for which we develop a theoretical model. This would manifest in a broad single peak associated with both H$_2$O and OH stretches and a low frequency peak appearing at elevated temperature.
summary: The researchers show that in CsOH.H2O, water and hydroxyl molecules are not stable entities but constantly interconvert through proton exchange. This process drives the structural transition of the crystal and provides a unique mechanism for high ionic conductivity without long-range molecular diffusion.
main_problem: Understanding the atomic-level dynamics, lifetime of water molecules, and the mechanism of the order-disorder transition in the crystal CsOH.H2O.
main_result: The order-disorder transition proceeds via a chemical reaction (proton exchange) rather than molecular rotation, where water and hydroxyl units interconvert on a picosecond scale.
method: Ab initio Molecular Dynamics (AIMD) using rSCAN and PBE functionals, combined with Density Functional Perturbation Theory (DFPT) and a 1D anharmonic Hamiltonian model for Raman spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The system is caesium hydroxide monohydrate (CsOH.H2O), featuring a quasi-2D honeycomb lattice of water and hydroxide ions that acts as a 2D analogue of water.
key_observables: Raman spectra (stretching, bending, and librational modes), radial distribution functions (RDF), mean squared displacements (MSD), and proton exchange barriers.
important_parameters: Temperature range (200K to 700K), proton exchange barrier (~0.2 eV), and picosecond-scale molecular lifetimes.
main_assumptions: The hydrogen arrangement obeys 'ice rules'; Raman activity is modeled using a linear polarizability and a 1D harmonic well perturbed by a Gaussian term.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the crystal structure, describes the AIMD simulation setup, analyzes the symmetry-breaking and energetic stability of the lattice, investigates the dynamical network of proton exchange, and concludes with a theoretical model for Raman signatures.
why_interesting: The study explores a 2D analogue of water and the dynamics of a chemical-reaction-driven phase transition, which relates to many-body dynamics and the stability of networked systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustrating the vicinal molecular dynamics cell. Top figure shows all 60/120 independent O/H atoms as a single layer. Lower figure shows side view of the layer in Cm, em- phasising the buckling. This structure was quenched from a 600K MD snapshot. It can be seen that the ice rules are fol- lowed, all oxygens can be assigned to OH or H2O molecules but the OH and H2O are somewhat disordered. Complete disorder is prohibited by the ice rules: it is possible to have an OH-only 6-membered ring, but two adjacent rings must contain at least one OH and one H2O. Cs atoms are omitted for clarity.
FIG. 2. Unstable I41amd structure with symmetry enforced a=b=4.329, c= 14.25, and minimum energy P1 equivalent with symmetry relaxed and phonons frozen in a=4.619, b= 4.566, c=15.717.

FIG. 3. Top left: The arrangement of OH and H2O molecules in the Cm structure. The OH molecules all point in the same direction. Top right: Alternative bonding with disor- der, showing an all-OH ring while still obeying the ice rules. Bottom left: Buckled layer from time averaged MD positions at 200K. Bottom right: Flat layer from time averaged MD positions at 300K.
FIG. 4. Partial RDFs at temperatures 200K, 300K, 400K, 500K, 600K, and 700K.

FIG. 5. Anharmonic potential model for H2O..OH mode. Top: potential and the associated wavefunctions for hydro- gen and deuterium. For these parameters, the ground state (blue) clearly corresponds to the hydrogen in the leftmost potential well: an OH bond. The first excited state can be regarded not as an excitation of this OH stretch, but rather the hydrogen bonded to the other hydrogen. The second ex- cited state is closer to an excitation of the OH stretch, Bot- tom: Contribution to Raman spectrum from this mode only, at a range of temperatures, using a range of bump heights and fundamental frequencies from 2391-2641 to represent the range of environments of the water donors and OH...
